ServiceFAQ.infoBoxBody: Have questions or want to explore possibilities? Feel free to reach out on LinkedIn or book a call.

defaults.q: How fast can you deliver a project?
defaults.a: Depending on complexity, MVPs can be delivered in 2–4 weeks, full products in 6–12 weeks. We commit to clear timelines upfront.

defaults.q: What happens if deadlines aren't met?
defaults.a: We guarantee our delivery commitments. If we miss a deadline, we work overtime at no extra cost until it's done.
Absolutely — we've worked with early-stage startups through to enterprise teams. We adapt our process to your stage and budget.

defaults.q: Do you offer support after launch?
defaults.a: Yes. We offer ongoing maintenance, monitoring, and growth packages so your product keeps improving after launch.

HomeFAQ.description: Have questions or want to explore possibilities? Feel free to reach out or book a call.

faqs.q: How fast can you deliver a project?
faqs.a: Depending on the scope, we can deliver an MVP in as little as 3–14 days. For larger projects, we work in tight 2-week sprints and keep you updated every step of the way with clear milestones.

faqs.q: What happens if deadlines aren't met?
faqs.a: We take delivery commitments seriously. If a deadline is at risk, we flag it early and work overtime at no extra cost to meet it. Transparent communication is core to how we operate.
Absolutely — startups are our sweet spot. We understand the pace and constraints of early-stage companies and can adapt to your runway, whether that means a lean MVP or a full product build.

faqs.q: Do you offer support after launch?
faqs.a: Yes. All of our engagements include 6 months of post-launch support. We're here to fix bugs, monitor performance, and iterate with you as your product grows.

FounderPage.name: Eliaz Bobadilla
FounderPage.intro: Founder and lead engineer at Bobadilla Tech. I build systems that ship and hold up — not prototypes dressed as products.

background.heading: Background
background.p1: I started writing code at 14. Not tutorials — actual projects. That early habit of building to understand (instead of reading to understand) still defines how I work.
background.p2: Over the past decade I've worked across early-stage startups, scale-up teams, and client projects spanning fintech, fitness, developer tooling, and infrastructure. Some of those shipped to thousands of users. Some got scrapped for the right reasons. All of them taught me something concrete.
background.p3: My focus has shifted progressively toward backend architecture, AI systems integration, and scalable infrastructure — areas where engineering decisions made early compound hard in both directions.

approach.heading: How I approach building software
approach.p1: I don't start with architecture. I start with the constraint. What's the real bottleneck — latency, cost, complexity, time to market? The answer changes the design more than any framework choice does.
approach.p2: I default toward simple and boring. That means reaching for proven tools before new ones, writing code that the next engineer can read without context, and treating every abstraction as debt until it proves its worth.
approach.p3: When AI is part of the stack, I treat it as an integration problem, not a research problem. The goal is reliable output, bounded behavior, and clear fallback logic — not a demo.

expect.heading: What to expect working with me

items.title: Direct scoping
items.body: I'll tell you what's achievable, what's not, and where the real unknowns are — before we start.

items.title: No middlemen
items.body: You talk to the engineer doing the work. There's no account manager between us.

items.title: Fast feedback loops
items.body: I ship early and often. You see real progress, not status updates.

items.title: Honest tradeoffs
items.body: If a simpler solution exists, I'll say so. Complexity should be justified, not default.

cta.heading: Want to talk through a project?
cta.body: 15 minutes. No pitch. Just an honest conversation about what you're building and whether we're a fit.

TeamPage.heading: A small team.
TeamPage.intro: Three senior engineers on every project. Specialists brought in when the work calls for it. No juniors learning on your budget.

core.heading: Core team
core.description: These three are consistent across every engagement. High ownership, direct communication, no hand-offs.

members.note: On every project. Sets technical direction and does the work.
Owns the client layer end-to-end. Strong opinions on UX.
Deep in the plumbing. Reliable on complex data models and edge cases.

extended.heading: Extended network
extended.description: Trusted specialists we've worked with across multiple projects. They come in when the scope requires their depth — not to pad headcount.

items.label: Mobile
items.detail: iOS and Android, native and cross-platform

items.label: Compliance
items.detail: Security review, audit prep, HIPAA / SOC2 context

items.heading: You get the right people, not the available ones
items.body: Large agencies staff projects with whoever's free. We staff projects with whoever fits. If a specialist isn't right for your work, they're not on it.

items.heading: No dilution
items.body: Senior engineers don't delegate to juniors then review at the end. The people you speak to are the people writing the code.

items.heading: Tight feedback loops
items.body: Three engineers who know each other's work can move fast and catch problems early. More people usually means slower cycles, not faster ones.

cta.heading: Want to meet the team?
cta.body: Book a short call. We'll walk through your project and tell you exactly who would be involved.

StartSmallPage.heading: Work together
StartSmallPage.intro: The best way to know if we're a fit is to do a small piece of real work together. A focused project — fixed scope, fixed price — so you can see how we think and what we ship before deciding anything larger.

items.heading: You see the actual quality
items.body: A small deliverable tells you more than any sales call. You see how we scope work, how we communicate, and what the code looks like — before any long-term commitment.

items.heading: The risk is low
items.body: Fixed scope, fixed price. If the output isn't what you expected, you're not locked in. Most clients who start small come back for more, but that's your call to make.

items.heading: We learn your codebase
items.body: Even a small project gives us real context on your stack, your constraints, and how your team works. That pays off immediately if we continue.

projects.heading: Example starter projects
projects.note: These are illustrative — we'll scope your specific work before quoting.

items.title: Feature build
items.description: A scoped, production-ready feature added to your existing codebase. Authentication flow, payment integration, notification system, dashboard widget — something real, something shipped.

items.title: API endpoint
items.description: A single API endpoint or small group of endpoints, fully implemented — validation, error handling, tests, documented. Not a scaffold. A finished piece of backend.

items.title: System audit
items.description: A technical review of your architecture, codebase, or infrastructure. We'll identify the real risks and bottlenecks, not a checklist. You get a written report and a follow-up call.

steps.title: Brief
steps.body: Tell us what you need. A short conversation or a written brief — either works.

steps.title: Quote
steps.body: We scope it, price it, and send you a fixed quote. No surprises after.

steps.title: Deliver
steps.body: We build it. You review it. We ship it. Typically 1–2 weeks.

cta.heading: Ready to start?
cta.body: Tell us what you need. We'll scope it and send you a fixed quote within 24 hours.
cta.note: No retainer. No minimum. Just the work.

CaseStudiesPage.subtitle: Two published so far. Real systems, real decisions, real tradeoffs.

studies.title: CompileStrength
studies.description: Full-stack subscription product with an AI agent that generates personalized workout programs. Built on Next.js, Cloudflare Workers, and Neon PostgreSQL. Shipped end-to-end — billing, auth, AI, and edge deployment included.

studies.title: Requiems API
studies.description: A production API platform exposing email validation, location, finance, and utility endpoints under a single key. Built as a multi-language monorepo — Go API, Rails dashboard, two Cloudflare Workers for auth and key management.

CompileStrengthPage.title: CompileStrength
CompileStrengthPage.intro: An AI-powered fitness SaaS. Users chat with an agent that generates personalized, science-based workout programs — and tracks sessions over time as training adapts.

overview.heading: Overview
overview.p1: CompileStrength is a subscription SaaS product for serious lifters who want programming grounded in training science, not generic templates. The core product is an AI agent that asks about training history, goals, and constraints — then generates a full periodized program.
overview.p2: Beyond generation, users track sessions, log progress, and watch their program adapt over time. The product includes a public calculator suite for acquisition and a subscription billing layer for monetization.

problem.heading: Problem
problem.p1: Fitness apps either give you rigid templates or put you in front of a generic chatbot. Neither produces a coherent, periodized program. The people who actually know programming — coaches — don't scale. The people who want good programming — intermediate and advanced lifters — don't have access to them.
problem.p2: The technical challenge was building an AI agent that produces structured, valid workout programs rather than conversational text. Output needed to be machine-readable, consistent, and safe to display — not just coherent prose.

solution.heading: Solution
solution.p1: We built the AI layer on Mastra — an agent framework that lets you define workflows with structured input/output schemas, not just free-form prompts. The workout compiler agent follows a fixed workflow: gather context, validate constraints, generate program blocks, validate output structure, return to the user.
solution.p2: Billing (LemonSqueezy) and auth (Better Auth) were integrated as first-class concerns from the start. The agent's generation limits are gated by subscription tier, and session tracking is tied to the user's program history — not a separate data model.

technicalApproach.heading: Technical approach
technicalApproach.p1: The entire backend runs on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext.js. This means no traditional server, no cold starts, and global edge distribution — but it also means a constrained runtime. The Node.js API surface available on Workers is limited, which ruled out several common libraries.
technicalApproach.p2: Neon PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM was chosen specifically for its HTTP-based driver, which works inside the Workers runtime where standard TCP database connections don't. Drizzle handles schema, migrations, and queries — all with full TypeScript inference.
technicalApproach.p3: State management (Zustand) is limited to client-side concerns: active workout session UI, animation state, user preferences. No server state is duplicated client-side.

items.items: Next.js 16 App Router, React 19, Tailwind CSS v4
Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext.js — edge-deployed, zero cold starts
Neon PostgreSQL (serverless) + Drizzle ORM with HTTP driver
Better Auth with Drizzle adapter (Cloudflare-compatible session handling)
Mastra agent framework + Google Gemini (workout compiler agent)
Zod throughout — API boundaries and form inputs

items.label: Fully subscription-ready — billing, auth, AI, and edge deploy all integrated
Cloudflare Workers edge runtime, globally distributed from launch
Agent generates valid, machine-readable programs — not conversational text

items.heading: Edge-first from day one
items.body: Running on Cloudflare Workers means no cold starts and global distribution without infrastructure management. The tradeoff is a constrained runtime — no Node.js APIs, limited native modules. We scoped the stack around that constraint, not against it.

items.heading: AI as a feature, not a proof of concept
items.body: The Mastra agent doesn't just call a model — it follows a structured workflow, validates outputs, and falls back gracefully when generation fails. Users don't see the model; they see a workout program.

items.heading: Billing integrated from the start
items.body: LemonSqueezy webhook handling was part of the initial build, not retrofitted. Subscription state flows into the UI and the AI agent's usage limits from the same source of truth.

cta.heading: Building something similar?
cta.body: AI products, SaaS subscriptions, edge-deployed systems — let's talk about your project.

RequiemsApiPage.title: Requiems API
RequiemsApiPage.intro: A production API platform that unifies email validation, location, finance, and utility APIs under a single key. Built as a multi-language monorepo with Go, Rails, and Cloudflare Workers.

overview.heading: Overview
overview.p1: Requiems API is a developer-facing API platform. One API key gives access to a growing set of categorized APIs — email validation, geographic data, financial utilities, text processing, and technology tools. The goal is to eliminate the sourcing, validation, and infrastructure overhead that comes with integrating multiple third-party data sources.
overview.p2: The platform includes a public-facing API (via a Cloudflare Worker gateway), a developer dashboard (Rails), a key management service, and a high-performance Go backend that handles all business logic.

problem.heading: Problem
problem.p1: Developers building real applications need access to reliable data APIs — email validation before user registration, timezone data for scheduling, currency rates for billing. The status quo is integrating five different APIs with five different SDKs, auth schemes, rate limit policies, and reliability profiles.
problem.p2: The technical challenge here was multi-dimensional: a single platform serving different API categories with different data characteristics, an auth layer that's fast enough to not add perceptible latency, and a management interface that doesn't require a full SPA deployment.

solution.heading: Solution
solution.p1: We chose a polyglot monorepo. Go handles the API core — it's fast, compiles to a small binary, and the concurrency model fits high-volume request handling. Rails handles the developer dashboard — it's batteries-included for admin UIs and Hotwire means no separate frontend build pipeline. Two Cloudflare Workers handle the public edge: one for auth and rate limiting, one for key management and analytics.
solution.p2: pnpm workspaces manages the JavaScript services. Docker Compose orchestrates the full stack locally — Go API, Rails, both Workers (via Wrangler), PostgreSQL, and Redis — from a single command.

items.name: Go API
items.detail: Chi router, pgx driver (jackc/pgx v5), golang-migrate for schema management, Sentry for error tracking. All API categories (email, location, finance, text, technology) are domain-separated. Fast, stateless, horizontally scalable.

items.name: Auth Gateway
items.detail: Cloudflare Worker built with Hono. Handles API key validation, rate limiting, and request proxying to the Go API. Cloudflare D1 tracks usage per key. OpenAPI spec generated from route definitions. This is the only service exposed publicly.

items.name: API Management Worker
items.detail: Second Cloudflare Worker for key issuance, usage exports, and developer analytics. Swagger UI is served directly from the Worker for interactive API exploration. Not publicly routed.

items.name: Rails 8 Dashboard
items.detail: Rails 8.1 with Hotwire (Turbo + Stimulus) for reactive pages without a separate frontend build. Tailwind CSS for styling. Used by developers managing keys and by admins monitoring usage. PostgreSQL via ActiveRecord.

items.heading: Auth at the edge, logic in Go
items.body: The Cloudflare Worker validates API keys and checks rate limits before a request ever reaches the Go API. D1 (SQLite at the edge) handles usage counters. This separation means the Go API only processes authenticated, rate-checked requests — it doesn't touch auth overhead.

items.heading: Redis for hot-path caching
items.body: Geographic lookups and financial data responses that are expensive to compute or unlikely to change within a time window are cached in Redis. The Go API handles cache-aside logic per API category with independent TTLs.

items.heading: Domain isolation in Go
items.body: Each API category (email, location, finance, etc.) is a separate domain package. This makes it straightforward to scale, test, or replace individual API categories without touching the others. The router wires domains together — it doesn't contain business logic.

items.heading: Rails without a SPA
items.body: Hotwire (Turbo + Stimulus) gives the dashboard real-time page updates without shipping a React app. The Rails server renders HTML; Turbo handles partial page replacement over websockets or fetch. This simplifies the deployment and removes the need for a separate frontend build.

items.name: Validation
items.detail: Email validation, disposable detection, MX lookup

items.name: Places
items.detail: Cities, postal codes, timezone, geocoding, holidays, working days

items.name: Finance
items.detail: Currency, exchange rates, financial data utilities

items.name: Technology
items.detail: QR code generation, password utilities, user agent parsing

items.name: Networking
items.detail: IP lookup, DNS resolution, network utilities

items.items: Go 1.26 — Chi router, pgx v5, golang-migrate
Redis (go-redis) — response caching, rate limit counters
Cloudflare Workers — Hono framework, TypeScript, Zod
Cloudflare D1 (SQLite) — usage tracking per API key
Rails 8.1 — Hotwire, Turbo, Stimulus, Tailwind CSS
PostgreSQL — shared between Go API and Rails dashboard
pnpm workspaces, Docker Compose for local development
Sentry (Go), structured logging, Biome for TS linting

items.label: Unified under one key, one SDK, one billing relationship
Key validation and rate limiting happen before requests reach Go — minimal latency overhead
Dashboard, key management, usage analytics, and Swagger docs — all shipped

cta.heading: Need a backend like this?
cta.body: API platforms, multi-service architectures, Go backends — let's talk about what you're building.
